blocks.content: This is what the house looked like at two in the morning on the first night.

From the outside: dark, mostly. A light on in one upstairs room — Nadia's, though anyone watching from the beach wouldn't have known whose. The curtains were open. The balcony door was latched. The light went out at two-fourteen.
From the lane at the front: six cars in the gravel, a welcome mat from the previous renters that the current ones hadn't noticed, a recycling bin someone had left at the curb two days ago.

From the inside: three people sleeping, two people somewhere between sleep and not, one person in the kitchen at two-oh-seven refilling her water glass and standing at the window for a moment before going back to bed.

And on the desk in the front bedroom — the one Marcus had taken because it was closest to the bathroom and he hadn't thought about it beyond that — the dark blue canvas bag, the zip pocket, the sealed envelope.
Everything was still. Everything looked ordinary.
This is always how it looks the night before.
